food_subgroup: Beef rice bowl with egg topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general gyudon / beef bowl entry; no specific code exists for the half boiled egg and spring onion variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific gyudon code. A0CKP (beef and rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for gyudon or this variant. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with simmered beef slices, a half boiled egg, spring onion, and Korean chilli pepper sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — composite nutrient estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice, simmered beef (short plate / chuck), whole egg (soft-boiled), spring onion, soy sauce, and mirin
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific gyudon variant. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition: approximately 55% steamed white rice, 25% simmered thinly sliced beef, 10% half boiled egg, 5% spring onion, and 5% sauce components (soy sauce, mirin, gochujang). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for each component. Values are per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Japanese-style) and beef bowl analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: Gyudon is predominantly steamed white rice (~55% by weight). The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 72 for boiled white rice (Japanese short-grain). The beef, egg, spring onion, and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. The dish GI is estimated at 72 (high) based on the dominant rice component. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 83.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = approximately 60. Available carbohydrate per serving estimated as 18.5 g/100g × 450 g = 83.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and Korean chilli sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit gyudon consumption or request reduced-sauce versions, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1710 mg sodium — over 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index driven by white rice base and added sugars in mirin and Korean chilli sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume gyudon cautiously due to its high GI (72) and high glycemic load per serving (~60), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction or substitution with brown rice is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and cholesterol from half boiled egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should be aware that a 450 g serving provides approximately 7.7 g saturated fat and 306 mg cholesterol, and should moderate frequency of consumption in the context of an overall heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg and Spring Onion Gyudon (半熟卵ねぎ牛丼, Hanjuku Tamago Negi Gyudon) is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish consisting of a base of steamed short-grain white rice topped with thinly sliced beef simmered in a sweet-savoury sauce of soy sauce, mirin, and dashi, garnished with fresh spring onion (negi), a half boiled (soft-set) egg, and a sweet-spicy Korean chilli pepper sauce (gochujang-style). Gyudon is one of Japan's most iconic fast-food dishes, popularised by chains such as Yoshinoya, Matsuya, and Sukiya. The dish is characterised by tender, umami-rich beef, the creamy richness of a runny-yolked half boiled egg, the mild sharpness of spring onion, and the heat of the Korean chilli sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content (~8.2 g/100g) from beef and egg, moderate fat (~4.5 g/100g), and a high carbohydrate load (~18.5 g/100g) dominated by white rice. Sodium content is high (~380 mg/100g) due to soy sauce and chilli sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The high glycemic index (GI ~72) and high glycemic load per serving (~60) make it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without portion control. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (soy sauce contains wheat) or fish (dashi). It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner meal in Japanese fast-food restaurants and is widely available in Singapore through chains such as Sukiya.
common_user_queries: What is half boiled egg gyudon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific gyudon variant with half boiled egg, spring onion, and Korean chilli sauce. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each component. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-defined and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the assembled dish, estimated by weighted ingredient composition (approximately 55% steamed white rice, 25% simmered beef, 10% half boiled egg, 5% spring onion, 5% sauce). 2. Sodium is high due to soy sauce and Korean chilli sauce; actual restaurant values may vary. 3. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant white rice component (University of Sydney GI Database, Japanese short-grain boiled white rice). 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as gyudon is a composite dish with rice, beef, and egg as primary components. 5. The input cuisine_tags included 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', which are not relevant to this dish and have been excluded. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly reported by a single source for this dish. 7. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all micronutrients have been estimated from component data. 8. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef from a non-certified source (Sukiya Singapore — halal certification status not confirmed at time of processing).

Corrected food_group from 'Dairy and eggs' (input error) to 'Mixed' reflecting composite dish nature.
Added Japanese local name 半熟卵ねぎ牛丼 and romanized form Hanjuku Tamago Negi Gyudon to satisfy G6 for Japanese cuisine.
Estimated nutrient values using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted ingredient composition methodology.
Assigned GI 72 from University of Sydney GI Database (Japanese boiled white rice) as analogous estimate for dominant rice component.
